_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 9-36R Naloxone (MDHHS Reviewed 2025). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Medication Protocol Section 9-36R: Naloxone — MDHHS Reviewed 2025
indications: Opioid overdose — respiratory depression or altered mental status from known or suspected opioid intoxication
Cardiac arrest — known or suspected opioid-related arrest
precautions: Naloxone has a shorter half-life than most opioids — patient may re-narcotize after naloxone wears off; transport and monitor closely
Rapid reversal may precipitate acute withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients: agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, vomiting — titrate to ventilatory effect, not full consciousness
Do NOT withhold naloxone based on concern for withdrawal — respiratory depression is the treatment priority
Repeat dosing may be required for synthetic opioids (fentanyl analogs, carfentanil) that are more potent — may need higher or more frequent doses
expected_effects: Reversal of opioid-induced respiratory depression within 2-5 minutes
Improved respiratory rate and tidal volume
Increased level of consciousness (do not aim for full alertness — titrate to ventilation)
side_effects: Acute opioid withdrawal (agitation, diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, hypertension)
Seizure (rare — in patients on chronic opioids with abrupt reversal)
notes: Preferred adult IN route: Narcan 4 mg nasal spray — one full spray in one nostril; repeat other nostril if no response in 3-5 minutes
Alternative adult IN via atomizer: naloxone 2 mg/2 mL via MAD — half per nostril
Cardiac arrest (known opioid): 2 mg IV/IO or 2-4 mg IN during resuscitation
Pediatric IN table (age-based): ≥36 months: 2 mg; 19-35 months: 1.5 mg; 3-18 months: 1 mg; 0-2 months: 0.5 mg

dosing.population: Adult (>14 yr) — opioid overdose IN (Narcan nasal spray)
dosing.dose: Narcan 4 mg nasal spray — one nostril; repeat other nostril if no response in 3-5 minutes
dosing.notes: Preferred adult IN method; each Narcan device = 4 mg

dosing.dose: Naloxone 2 mg/2 mL via MAD — 1 mL each nostril (1 mg per nostril)
dosing.notes: Alternative IN method using naloxone injectable via MAD

dosing.population: Adult (>14 yr) — opioid overdose IM or slow IV
dosing.dose: Naloxone 2 mg IM or slow IV
dosing.notes: Slow IV to reduce acute withdrawal risk; titrate to respiratory effect
During CPR for suspected opioid-related arrest

dosing.dose: ≥36 months: 2 mg; 19-35 months: 1.5 mg; 3-18 months: 1 mg; 0-2 months: 0.5 mg
dosing.notes: Via MAD; split dose half per nostril when possible

dosing.population: Pediatric — opioid overdose IM or IV
